title: Generics
summary: Write type-safe, reusable code using Java generics with type parameters

sections.title: Generics as Adaptable Containers
sections.content: Imagine a universal phone charger with interchangeable tips — the same charger base works with USB-C, Lightning, or Micro-USB depending on which tip you attach. Java generics work the same way: you write a class or method once, and the user decides what type it works with. Instead of writing separate classes for IntegerList, StringList, and DoubleList, you write one List<T> where T is a placeholder for any type. This gives you compile-time type safety, meaning the compiler catches type mistakes before your program runs — like a charger that physically prevents plugging in the wrong cable.

sections.title: A Generic Class and Method
sections.content: This generic Box class stores any type, and a generic method swaps array elements

sections.title: Bounded Type Parameters and Wildcards
sections.content: Sometimes you want to restrict what types T can be — like saying 'T must be a type of Animal' rather than just 'T can be anything'. Bounded type parameters do this: <T extends Number> means T can only be Number or its subclasses (Integer, Double, etc.). Wildcards (? extends Number) are used when you don't care about the exact type as long as it's a subtype of Number. The PECS rule (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) helps you decide: use ? extends T when you only read from a collection, and ? super T when you only write to it.

sections.title: Using raw types instead of parameterized types
sections.content: Most common mistake: writing List list = new ArrayList(); instead of List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();. Raw types bypass generic type checking, allowing you to add any object and causing ClassCastException at runtime. Fix: always use parameterized types. Wrong: List names = new ArrayList(); names.add(123);. Correct: List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Create a generic method called findMax that takes an array of Comparable<T> elements and returns the largest element according to natural ordering. The method signature should use a bounded type parameter. Test it with an Integer array and a String array.
hints: What keyword bounds T to only types that implement Comparable?
Which method in the Comparable interface compares two elements?
How do you iterate through the array to find the maximum value?
